FAIR - Centre for Experimental Research on Fairness, Inequality and Rationality (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)

rspStmt.AuthEnty: Alexander W. Cappelen (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)

rspStmt.AuthEnty: Bertil Tungodden (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)
rspStmt.othId: Bertil Tungodden (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)

producer.affiliation: FAIR - Centre for Experimental Research on Fairness, Inequality and Rationality (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)

prodStmt.producer: FAIR - Centre for Experimental Research on Fairness, Inequality and Rationality (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)
prodStmt.fundAg: Centre of Excellence funding from the Research Council of Norway (2017–2026)

distStmt.distrbtr: FAIR - Centre for Experimental Research on Fairness, Inequality and Rationality (NHH Norwegian School of Economics)

abstract.p: This paper investigates fairness perceptions of extreme income inequality generated in winner-take-all competitions. Two large-scale experiments with more than 7,000 participants from the general population of the U.S. show that extreme earnings inequality is widely accepted, even when the winner only slightly outperforms the runner-up. The effect of the winning margin on inequality acceptance is modest compared to the effect of shifting the source of inequality from luck to winning by the smallest possible margin. The experimental choices are systematically associated with broader fairness attitudes and policy preferences, including support for higher taxation of top earners and redistributive economic policy.

sumDscr.universe: General population of the United States

dataColl.sampProc: Three separate experiments totalling more than 7,000 participants. (1) mmwinner: 349 participants recruited from the student population at NHH Norwegian School of Economics, 2014. (2) mmwinner_RN2018: 4,000 participants from a representative national sample, 2018. (3) mmwinner_Prolific2025: 3,005 participants recruited online via Prolific, 2025.
dataColl.collMode: Incentivized spectator experiment across three studies using different recruitment modes. (1) Laboratory sessions with in-person participants; (2) Representative national online survey panel; (3) Online experiment via Prolific. In all studies, two workers competed in a problem-solving task; spectators observed performance and earnings and made allocation decisions with actual monetary stakes.

othrStdyMat.relMat:
Replication code and data
GitHub Repository
